title: Big rebates for over-65s good for taxpayers
full_text: Other Editions 29 April 2026 TheNation Big rebates for over-65s good for taxpayers The Australian NATASHA ROBINSON, HEALTH EDITOR 29 April 2026 759 words English AUSTLN 4 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. There are growing questions as to the rationale for the federal government’s decision to axe higher health insurance rebates for older people, with more analysis indicating subsidies to older people yield big savings for taxpayers, described as a “very good financial deal” for government. Last week Health Minister Mark Butler announced his intention to abolish higher means-tested private health insurance rebates for people over 65 and 70 in the upcoming budget, arguing the Howard-era policy did not make policy sense and the change would reap $3bn in savings across four years that would be redirected to aged care. But previous analysis considered by the federal health department found that paying rebates to older people for their health insurance premiums, calculated at the higher rate, represented a net saving to the government of almost $5000 for each individual aged over 75. This is largely because older people who drop or downgrade their private insurance will instead be forced to use public hospitals for treatment, at a cost to taxpayers. Pensioners now face having to stump up hundreds of extra dollars for their private health insurance as a result of the lowering of the rebates. Private Healthcare Australia has estimated axing the higher rebates will cost about 1.4 million older people up to $640 a year depending on their level of coverage. The federal government has not revealed the detailed policy basis for its decision on axing the higher rebates but insists they are unfair to younger people. But consultation on private health subsidies has been occurring intensively in recent years. In 2024, the federal health department held consultations on private health subsidies and incentives and released for public consultation several reports originally commissioned under the former Coalition government examining whether rebates represented value for money. One of the reports specifically examined whether premium rebates for consumers as well as the Medicare Levy Surcharge represented a net cost to government or a saving. The report by consultancy NEAA Associates found paying private health insurance rebates to older people, especially those aged over 75, represented the “greatest savings” for government, far higher than any other age group. Currently, people aged over 70 get the highest rebates and singles aged between 65 and 69 who have an ­income of less than $101,000 receive a rebate towards the cost of their health insurance premium of 28.33 per cent, The rebate also applies to someone over 65 who is in a family with an income of less than $202,000. The rebate is higher for those aged over 70 in this income tier, rising to 32.38 per cent. Those under 65 receive a rebate of 24.3 per cent. The NEAA report estimated the total offset to government of paying rebates to all ages at all income tiers. The offset represents the healthcare costs saved because a privately insured individual otherwise would have used the public health system. “Our estimates show large offsets for the government, with a nationally representative population average of $1434 per person,” the report found. “For most age and income groups, the net cost (of PHI rebates) is negative. Indeed, only for the 18-24 (also for singles), 35-44 age groups, the net cost is positive, meaning subsidies are higher than avoided costs for those groups. “In particular … the greatest savings for the government come from the single, older (75+) and individuals in the range $105-$140k (an equivalent group in the family panel). On average, savings for that group are $4891 (and $4792, respectively). “Older individuals are more likely to make use of health serv­ices, and therefore greater offsets. Subsidies these older individuals receive are small in relation to the costs they avoid in the public system. We conclude that the current PHI subsidy policy is a very good financial deal for the government.” Mr Butler says the NEAA report was commissioned by the previous government and he is now relying on new modelling with different inputs and conclusions. The government’s modelling indicates that the number of people expected to cancel their private health insurance policies because of the axing of higher rebates was likely to be about 0.4 per cent of the total market. The number of people privately insured is currently growing at about 2 per cent a year. “It will have a very, very minimal impact and had no policy merit,” Mr Butler said last week. summary: The Australian government plans to abolish higher health insurance rebates for individuals over 65, which Health Minister Mark Butler claims will save $3 billion over four years. However, analysis indicates that these rebates actually provide significant savings to taxpayers by reducing public hospital costs for older individuals. The decision has raised concerns about the financial impact on approximately 1.4 million older Australians who may face increased health insurance costs.
publication:
Australia lacks national strategy to regulate AI spread in workplace, report states
Australian Broadcasting Corporation News28 April 20261,245 wordsnational work reporter Bronwyn Herb

Australia risks repeating the mistakes it made with social media unless it moves quickly to regulate the spread of artificial intelligence in the workplace, according to a new report.
key_points: The government aims to save $3 billion by cutting higher health insurance rebates for those over 65.
Previous analysis shows that these rebates yield significant savings for taxpayers by reducing reliance on public hospitals.
Approximately 1.4 million older Australians could face increased costs of up to $640 per year due to the changes.
relevance: This issue is pertinent for actuaries and financial services professionals as it impacts health insurance pricing and the financial sustainability of aged care funding.
